planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in fall for spring germination, or in early spring; seeds require light to germinate and must not be covered. Silver mullein forms a large, flat basal rosette in year one and sends up a towering flower spike in year two. It self-sows freely in appropriate conditions to maintain a colony. The cultivar Arctic Summer is an improved selection with large, bright-yellow flowers on pure-white woolly stems.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in lean, well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; silver mullein is one of the most visually striking biennials available, distinguished by its extraordinary silver-white woolly texture that covers every surface — leaves, stems, flower buds, and bracts — in a dense layer of fine, silky, white hairs that make the entire plant appear frosted or dipped in silver. In the first year it forms a large, flat, dramatically architectural basal rosette of broadly oval, felted-white leaves that can reach 2 feet across. In the second year, a stout, silvery, candelabra-branched flower spike rises 4-7 feet bearing densely packed, bright-yellow flowers with purple-pink stamens against the silver stems — a striking color contrast. Native to rocky, dry, alkaline slopes of northwestern Turkey, it thrives in very well-drained, lean soils where excess moisture does not cause crown rot. A magnificent structural plant for the dry border, gravel garden, or as a seasonal focal point surrounded by lower-growing perennials.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or late summer in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; rosy-leaf sage is damaged below 28 degrees F and killed at 25 degrees F. In zones 8-11 it overwinters as a perennial and grows as a substantial shrubby plant; in colder zones it can be grown as a large annual or lifted and overwintered frost-free. It blooms very late — September through November — when most other flowering plants have finished.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; rosy-leaf sage is a tall, shrubby Mexican salvia producing some of the most unusually beautiful flowers in the genus — the large, prominently inflated pink-carmine calyx tubes (the involucre that gives it its name) persist and enlarge to become ornamental bracts that frame the rosy-pink to carmine flower tubes, giving each flower cluster a double-layered, large-scale, almost peony-like appearance quite unlike the typical small-flowered salvia. The flowers appear in terminal clusters in September through November — later than almost any other salvia — making it an invaluable late-season nectar source for migrating hummingbirds. In zones 8-11 it grows as a substantial perennial shrub 4-6 feet tall; in zones 6-7 it can be grown as a late-season annual in a large container. The cultivar Bethellii has particularly large flower clusters and is the most widely grown form.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or summer in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Darcy's sage is hardier than most scarlet salvias and can survive brief dips to about 20 degrees F when established. In zones 8-10 it grows as a long-lived perennial subshrub. Prune back by one-third in early spring to maintain shape and encourage vigorous new growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Darcy's sage is a compact Mexican native from the Sierra Madre Oriental of Nuevo Leon and Coahuila producing an exceptionally long season of brilliant, clear scarlet-red flowers — a deeper, truer red than the salmon-to-pink range of most Salvia greggii cultivars — from spring through frost. The flowers are two-lipped and slightly larger than those of autumn sage, making it particularly effective for hummingbirds. Native to dry, rocky, limestone slopes at 8,000-10,000 feet elevation, it is adapted to hot, dry, alkaline conditions and proves drought-tolerant once established. It was recently discovered by plant explorers and introduced to cultivation in the 1990s, and its combination of true scarlet color, extended bloom season, and drought tolerance has made it a favorite in the heat-tolerant perennial border. Exceptionally attractive to hummingbirds throughout its very long bloom period from April through October in mild climates.

planting.note: Divide the fleshy, tuberous roots in early spring before growth emerges, or plant container-grown plants in humus-rich, moist but well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part shade; Roscoea emerges very late in spring — often not until June — so mark the location carefully to avoid disturbing the roots. Plant the tuberous roots 4-6 inches deep to protect them from cold. Best in cool, humid climates; struggles in hot, dry summers.

directions: Plant tuberous roots 4-6 inches deep in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part shade; purple roscoea is a remarkable, late-emerging perennial from the Himalayas of Nepal and Tibet that produces flowers of such intricate beauty they are consistently compared to orchids — three-lipped, hooded, violet-purple flowers on short stems clasped by neat, strap-shaped, tropical-looking leaves. Each flower has a large, flared lower lip and two smaller lateral petals below a broad, hooded upper petal that together create a genuinely orchid-like appearance rare in cold-hardy garden plants. It is a member of the ginger family (Zingiberaceae) and, like the gingers, produces fleshy tuberous roots that store nutrients through the winter dormancy. The plant emerges extremely late in spring — often not until June — making it ideal for interplanting with spring bulbs whose foliage has already faded. Perfect for the shaded rock garden, woodland border, and any partly shaded position with cool, humus-rich, consistently moist but not waterlogged soil.

planting.note: Sow seeds after last frost in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; pipicha is a warm-season annual herb grown for the fresh culinary use of its pungent, narrow leaves. In zones 9-11 it can be grown as a short-lived perennial. Harvest leaves frequently to delay flowering and extend the harvest season. The leaves are most aromatic when harvested young.

directions: Sow seeds after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; pipicha is a strongly aromatic Mexican herb native to the dry, mountainous regions of Oaxaca, Puebla, and Guerrero, used in Oaxacan cuisine as one of the distinctive flavoring herbs — recognizable by its very narrow, linear leaves dotted with visible oil glands (the 'porophyllum' = pore-leaf name refers to these), which release a pungent, complex aroma combining elements of rue, cilantro, and anise when bruised or used in cooking. It is used in tlayudas, quesillo dishes, soups, black beans, and as a fresh herb scattered over completed dishes. Pipicha is closely related to papalo (Porophyllum ruderale) which is already more widely known in US markets, but has narrower, more pungent leaves and a slightly different flavor profile preferred by cooks from the Oaxacan Valleys. In regions with good Mexican grocery supply, both pipicha and papalo can sometimes be found fresh. Grow as a warm-season annual in most of North America.
